summary: Gruber analyzes Bloomberg's report that Apple is nearing a ~$1 billion/year deal to use Google's Gemini model to power the next-generation Siri, running on Apple's Private Cloud Compute servers. He argues this isn't 'settling' — the top LLM models are close enough in quality that choosing Gemini over Anthropic's Claude for financial reasons is like picking between a Mercedes and a BMW when you're getting a better deal on one. He notes the deal is a bargain given Google's $20B+ annual search payments to Apple and $100B+ AI investment. Gruber speculates that neither company will publicize the arrangement, allowing Apple to apply its own restrictive guardrails to Gemini while offering full branded Gemini as a separate extension, and preserving Apple's ability to switch providers silently in the future.
key_points: Apple choosing Gemini over Anthropic's Claude for financial reasons is still pursuing excellence — the top LLM models are all within striking distance of each other, while Siri is at least two generations behind all of them.

At $1B/year, the deal is remarkable value for Apple: it's effectively a 5% reduction in the $20B+ Google pays Apple annually for Safari search traffic, giving Apple access to a model built on $100B+ in AI investment.
Google may have agreed to favorable terms as a strategic play to keep OpenAI and Anthropic out of Apple's ecosystem.
Gruber envisions a two-tier system: a guardrailed white-label Gemini powering baseline Apple Intelligence, plus full branded Gemini available as an optional extension like ChatGPT is today.
Keeping the deal private benefits both companies — Apple can switch providers silently, and Google doesn't have to explain why branded Gemini is more capable than the Apple-constrained version.
money_quotes: It's more like choosing between a Mercedes and a BMW, and maybe you like the Mercedes a little more after test-driving both, but you're getting a way, way better deal from the BMW dealer so that's the one you buy.
Apple getting access to the fruits of that labor for $1 billion per year seems like such a steal that it makes me wonder why Google agreed to it.
Perhaps Google sees a strategic advantage to keeping competitors like OpenAI and Anthropic out of Apple's arms.

Today's top LLM models get weird when they're constrained by guardrails outside of their training. They want to do what they're prompted to do, in the way that information 'wants' to be free, and water 'wants' to run downhill.

Dams are hard to engineer, and require professional supervision. Apple is going to put PG-rated constraints on Apple Intelligence, but needs to power it using an underlying model that is technically capable of X-rated output, because that's what's available.
What Apple needs is a version of Apple Intelligence that isn't stupid, is reliable and dependable for a broad baseline of tasks and queries, and that users can trust to be utterly private.
By keeping this Gemini deal private, Apple can easily switch to another white-label provider or its own homegrown models in the future, without having to even mention it, let alone explain it.
key_insight: Apple paying Google $1B/year for a white-label Gemini is a strategic bargain that lets Apple leapfrog Siri's AI gap while maintaining privacy, guardrail control, and the freedom to switch providers invisibly.
